title: Hands-On Maintenance, Engine Theory & Diagnostic Thinking

required.title: [Equipment] Floor jack (3-ton minimum for cars, heavier for trucks), jack stands (pair, rated for the vehicle weight), wheel chocks

[Tools] Socket set (metric and SAE), combination wrench set, oil filter wrench (strap or cap type for your vehicle), drain pan, funnel, torque wrench, brake caliper tool (C-clamp or caliper piston tool), wire brush, brake cleaner
[Consumables] Engine oil (correct weight and quantity for your vehicle — check owner's manual), oil filter (correct part number for your vehicle), brake pads (correct for your vehicle)
[Equipment] OBD-II scanner (basic code reader, $20-50, or a Bluetooth OBD-II adapter with a smartphone app)
[Small engine] A single-cylinder four-stroke engine from a lawn mower, generator, or similar — can be a non-running engine purchased inexpensively for disassembly practice

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: ChrisFix — "How to Change Your Oil" and "How to Replace Brake Pads"
[Video] YouTube: Smarter Every Day — "How a Gasoline Engine Works"

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Haynes or Chilton repair manual for your specific vehicle

competence_markers.description: The student can lift a vehicle safely using a floor jack and jack stands, following the complete safety procedure
The student has performed a complete oil change on a real vehicle with correct procedure, fluid type, and leak check
The student has replaced brake pads on a real vehicle and can explain the hydraulic braking system
The student has disassembled and reassembled a small engine and can explain the four-stroke cycle from direct experience
The student can read OBD-II codes and understands that codes are starting points for diagnosis, not final answers
The student can articulate the economics of preventive maintenance — routine maintenance versus the cost of the failure it prevents
The student inspects belts and hoses and can identify wear patterns that indicate impending failure

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned what a vehicle is made of and what its fluids do. Now you put your hands on it. You are going to change oil, replace brake pads, work on a small engine, and begin diagnosing problems with an OBD-II scanner. These are the skills that transform you from someone who knows about cars to someone who can maintain and repair them.

Oil changes are the gateway skill. They are simple enough to build confidence and complex enough to require a real procedure: lifting the vehicle safely, locating the drain plug, draining the oil, replacing the filter, refilling with the correct amount and type, and checking for leaks. When you complete your first oil change, you will have done something that most adults pay someone else to do — and you will have done it correctly, safely, and with understanding.

Brake work is the first safety-critical repair. Brakes are what prevent a 4,000-pound machine from being an uncontrollable weapon. When you replace brake pads, you are not just swapping parts — you are taking responsibility for the stopping power of a vehicle that people's lives depend on. This is where 2.2 and IC1 (Character) meet most directly: careless brake work can kill someone. The standard is not "good enough." The standard is "right."

You will also disassemble a small engine — a lawn mower or generator engine — and see the four-stroke cycle with your own hands. Intake, compression, power, exhaust. Every gasoline engine in the world runs on this cycle. When you have held a piston, turned a crankshaft, and seen the valves open and close, the engine is no longer theory. It is mechanical reality that you have touched.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Oil change competence** — performing a complete oil change safely and correctly on a real vehicle
2. **Brake service** — replacing brake pads and inspecting rotors, understanding the hydraulic braking system
3. **Small engine theory** — disassembling and reassembling a single-cylinder engine to understand the four-stroke cycle at a visceral level
4. **Belt and hose inspection** — identifying wear patterns and understanding failure consequences
5. **OBD-II diagnostic introduction** — reading and interpreting diagnostic trouble codes as the starting point (not the final answer) for diagnosis
6. **Preventive maintenance economics** — understanding that routine maintenance costs a fraction of the failures it prevents
7. **Safe vehicle lifting** — using jacks and jack stands correctly, understanding the lethal consequences of improper lifting
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Lifting a Vehicle Safely

Before you can work under a vehicle, you must lift it safely. This is a life-or-death skill. A vehicle that falls off a jack while you are under it will crush you. This is not an exaggeration. People die every year from vehicles falling off jacks.

**The rules:**
- **Never work under a vehicle supported only by a jack.** A hydraulic jack can fail — a seal leaks, the mechanism slips, the vehicle shifts. Jack stands are solid steel supports that hold the weight mechanically. Once the jack stands are in place, the vehicle cannot fall even if the jack fails completely.
- **Work on a flat, solid surface.** Concrete or asphalt. Never on gravel, grass, or a slope. The jack can sink into soft surfaces or the vehicle can roll on a slope.
- **Chock the wheels** that remain on the ground. Place wheel chocks (blocks of wood or rubber wedges) behind the wheels that are not being lifted to prevent rolling.
- **Set the parking brake** before lifting.
- **Place jack stands at designated jack points.** Your vehicle's owner's manual identifies the reinforced points on the frame or body where jack stands should be placed. Placing them at non-designated points can crush body panels or damage the vehicle structure.

**The procedure:**
1. Park on flat, solid surface. Set parking brake. Place chocks.
2. Position the floor jack under the designated jack point.
3. Raise the vehicle until the wheel you need to access is off the ground.
4. Place jack stands at the designated support points.
5. Slowly lower the jack until the vehicle rests on the jack stands.
6. Shake the vehicle gently to confirm it is stable on the stands.
7. Only then — get under the vehicle.

### Part 2: Oil Change — Your First Maintenance Procedure

An oil change is the most fundamental vehicle maintenance procedure. It is also the most cost-effective: a $40 oil change prevents a $5,000 engine replacement.

**Preparation:**
- Determine the correct oil type, weight, and quantity for your vehicle (check the owner's manual). Example: 5W-30 synthetic, 5 quarts.
- Purchase the correct oil filter (look up the part number for your vehicle year, make, and model).
- Gather tools: socket set, oil filter wrench, drain pan, funnel, shop rags.
- Run the engine for 5 minutes to warm the oil — warm oil flows faster and carries more contaminants with it. Then shut it off.

**The procedure:**
1. Lift the vehicle and place on jack stands (or use ramps if available).
2. Position the drain pan under the oil drain plug (a bolt on the bottom of the oil pan, the lowest point of the engine).
3. Remove the drain plug with the correct socket. Oil will flow out — it will be warm, so be careful. Let it drain completely (5-10 minutes).
4. While the oil is draining, locate the oil filter. It is a cylindrical canister screwed onto the engine block. Use the oil filter wrench to unscrew it. More oil will come out — position the drain pan to catch it.
5. Prepare the new filter: apply a thin film of fresh oil to the rubber gasket on the new filter (this helps it seal properly and makes removal easier next time).
6. Screw the new filter on by hand — tighten it firmly but do not overtighten. Hand-tight plus 3/4 turn is the typical specification.
7. Replace the drain plug. Tighten it firmly with the socket — again, do not overtighten. A stripped drain plug is a common and expensive beginner mistake.
8. Lower the vehicle.
9. Pour the correct amount of fresh oil through the oil fill cap on top of the engine, using a funnel.
10. Start the engine and let it run for 30 seconds. The oil pressure light should turn off within a few seconds — this means the oil is circulating.
11. Shut off the engine. Wait 2 minutes for the oil to settle. Check the dipstick. Add oil if needed to reach the "full" mark.
12. Check under the vehicle for leaks at the drain plug and oil filter. If either is dripping, tighten slightly.
13. Dispose of the used oil properly — take it to an auto parts store or recycling center. Never pour used oil on the ground, down a drain, or in the trash.

### Part 3: Brake Pad Replacement

Brakes are a hydraulic system. When you press the brake pedal, you pressurize brake fluid, which pushes pistons in the brake calipers, which press brake pads against the brake rotors (discs attached to the wheels), which creates friction, which slows the vehicle.

Brake pads are the sacrificial component — they wear down over time so that the rotor (which is much more expensive) does not. When the pads wear thin, they must be replaced.

**The procedure (one wheel at a time):**
1. Lift the vehicle and place on jack stands. Remove the wheel (loosen lug nuts before lifting, then remove by hand once the wheel is off the ground).
2. Locate the brake caliper — it wraps around the rotor like a clamp. It is held by two bolts (caliper slide bolts or guide pins).
3. Remove the caliper bolts and lift the caliper off the rotor. Do not let it hang by the brake hose — support it with a wire or bungee cord hung from the suspension. The brake hose is a flexible hydraulic line, and hanging the caliper by it can damage it.
4. Remove the old brake pads from the caliper bracket.
5. Inspect the rotor. Run your finger across the surface — if you feel deep grooves, the rotor may need to be resurfaced or replaced. If the rotor has a visible lip at the edge, it is worn significantly. Measure the rotor thickness with a caliper if possible and compare to the minimum specification (stamped on the rotor or in the service manual).
6. Compress the caliper piston. The new pads are thicker than the worn-out ones, so the piston must be pushed back into the caliper to make room. Use a C-clamp or caliper piston tool. Press slowly and evenly. Note: on some vehicles (especially rear calipers with parking brake mechanisms), the piston must be rotated while pressing — check the service manual.
7. Install the new pads in the caliper bracket. They should seat firmly with the friction material facing the rotor.
8. Slide the caliper back over the rotor and new pads. Reinstall and tighten the caliper bolts.
9. Reinstall the wheel. Tighten lug nuts in a star pattern (not circular) to ensure even pressure.
10. Lower the vehicle. Torque the lug nuts to specification with a torque wrench.
11. **Before driving:** Pump the brake pedal several times until it feels firm. The first few presses will feel soft because the piston has been pushed back and needs to re-establish contact with the new pads. If you drive without doing this, you will have no brakes for the first pedal press.
12. Test drive at low speed in a safe area. Brakes should feel firm and stop the vehicle smoothly. New pads may squeal slightly for the first few stops — this is normal as the pad surfaces bed in.

### Part 4: The Four-Stroke Cycle — Small Engine Disassembly

A single-cylinder engine from a lawn mower or generator is the ideal teaching engine. It demonstrates every principle of the four-stroke cycle at a scale you can handle with basic tools.

**Disassembly:** Remove the shroud (sheet metal cover), air filter, carburetor, muffler/exhaust, and spark plug to expose the cylinder head. Remove the head bolts and lift the head off to reveal the piston and cylinder bore. Rotate the crankshaft by hand (using the blade or starter mechanism) and watch the piston move up and down.

**The four strokes:**
1. **Intake:** The piston moves down, creating a vacuum. The intake valve opens. Air and fuel are drawn into the cylinder through the carburetor.
2. **Compression:** Both valves close. The piston moves up, compressing the fuel-air mixture into a small space at the top of the cylinder. Compressing the mixture makes it burn more completely and more powerfully.
3. **Power:** The spark plug fires, igniting the compressed mixture. The burning gas expands rapidly, pushing the piston down with great force. This is the only stroke that produces power — the other three are preparation and cleanup.
4. **Exhaust:** The exhaust valve opens. The piston moves up, pushing the burned gases out of the cylinder through the exhaust port.

Then the cycle repeats. In a running engine, this happens hundreds or thousands of times per minute.

**What you learn by touching it:** The piston fits tightly in the cylinder bore — you can feel the compression rings gripping the cylinder wall. The crankshaft converts the piston's up-and-down motion into rotation. The flywheel stores rotational energy to carry the crankshaft through the three non-power strokes. The valves open and close with precise timing controlled by the camshaft. Every part has a purpose, and the purpose becomes obvious when you hold the part in your hand and move it through its range of motion.

**Reassembly:** Put it back together. Replace the head gasket (if you have a new one — the old one may be reusable on a practice engine). Torque the head bolts in the correct sequence and to the correct specification. Reinstall the spark plug, carburetor, air filter, exhaust, and shroud. If the engine was running before disassembly, it should run again when you are done. If it does not — diagnose why.

### Part 5: OBD-II Diagnostics Introduction

Every car manufactured since 1996 has an OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics) port — a standardized connector, usually under the dashboard on the driver's side. The vehicle's computer continuously monitors engine and emissions systems and stores diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs) when it detects a problem.

**Reading codes:** Plug the OBD-II scanner into the port. Turn the ignition on (engine can be off). Follow the scanner's menu to read stored codes.

**What codes tell you — and what they do not:** A code like P0301 (Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected) tells you the computer detected a misfire in cylinder 1. It does not tell you why. The misfire could be caused by a failed spark plug, a bad ignition coil, a clogged fuel injector, a compression problem, or several other issues. The code is the starting point for diagnosis — it points you to the right system and the right cylinder. You must then reason through the possible causes and test each one.

**Clearing codes:** After a repair, you can clear the stored codes with the scanner. If the problem was actually fixed, the code will not return. If it returns, the repair did not address the root cause.

**The economics of OBD-II literacy:** When your check engine light comes on, a mechanic will read the codes (often charges $50-100 for this alone) and then propose repairs. If you can read the codes yourself (a basic scanner costs $20-50), you arrive at the shop already knowing what the computer detected. You can research the most common causes, evaluate the mechanic's diagnosis, and avoid paying for unnecessary work. This is automotive literacy as financial literacy.

### Part 6: Preventive Maintenance Economics

Every vehicle has a maintenance schedule: oil changes, fluid flushes, belt replacement, brake service, filter replacement, tire rotation, and more. Each item costs a modest amount when performed on schedule. Each item, when neglected, eventually causes a failure that costs dramatically more.

- **Oil change:** $40 every 5,000-7,500 miles. Neglect: engine failure, $5,000-8,000.
- **Timing belt replacement:** $500-800 at 60,000-100,000 miles (on vehicles with timing belts). Neglect: belt breaks, valves hit pistons, engine destroyed, $3,000-5,000.
- **Coolant flush:** $100-150 every 30,000-50,000 miles. Neglect: corrosion, overheating, head gasket failure, $1,500-3,000.
- **Brake pad replacement:** $150-300 per axle. Neglect: pads wear to metal, rotors destroyed, $600-1,000+ per axle.
- **Transmission fluid change:** $150-250 every 30,000-60,000 miles. Neglect: transmission failure, $3,000-5,000.

The pattern is clear: preventive maintenance is dramatically cheaper than the failure it prevents. This is discipline (IC2) made financial: the person who changes oil on schedule, replaces belts on time, and addresses small problems before they become large ones spends a fraction of what the person who neglects maintenance spends. This connects directly to 4.1 (Financial Independence) — vehicle maintenance is one of the largest recurring expenses in adult life, and mechanical literacy directly reduces that cost.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Vehicle Lifting
**Developing well:** The student follows the complete lifting procedure every time: flat surface, parking brake, chocks, jack at designated point, jack stands at designated points, shake test before going under. The behavior is automatic and consistent.

**Developing slowly:** The student skips steps, places jack stands at incorrect points, or goes under the vehicle before the shake test.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you demonstrated the consequences of improper lifting? (Show the student a video of a vehicle falling off a jack — the visual impact reinforces the seriousness.) Have you personally verified jack stand placement on every occasion?

### 2. Oil Change
**Developing well:** The student performs the complete oil change procedure correctly: warm the engine, lift safely, drain, filter replacement (with gasket oiled), drain plug reinstalled and properly tightened, correct oil type and quantity, leak check, proper disposal. The student can explain each step's purpose.

**Developing slowly:** The student performs the mechanical steps but does not understand their purpose. Tightening is inconsistent (over-tight or under-tight). Steps are missed without a checklist.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student use a checklist? Even experienced mechanics use checklists for routine procedures. Provide one initially and wean off it as the procedure becomes automatic. Has the student performed enough oil changes? (One is introduction; three builds competence.)

### 3. Brake Service
**Developing well:** The student replaces pads correctly, inspects the rotor, compresses the piston properly, reassembles without binding, pumps the pedal until firm, and can explain the hydraulic system. The student understands that this is safety-critical work and treats it with appropriate seriousness.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats brake work casually, does not inspect the rotor, does not pump the pedal before driving, or cannot explain how hydraulic brakes work.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you communicated the seriousness of brake work clearly enough? The student must understand that a brake failure caused by their work could kill someone. This is not intended to create anxiety — it is intended to create the gravity that safety-critical work demands.

### 4. Engine Theory
**Developing well:** After disassembly and reassembly, the student can explain the four-stroke cycle by pointing to the parts involved. They can explain what each component does (piston, crankshaft, valves, camshaft, spark plug, flywheel) and why it matters.

**Developing slowly:** The student completed the disassembly mechanically but cannot explain the cycle or identify part functions.

**Diagnostic questions:** Did the student handle each part individually and discuss its function before moving to the next? Did you pause at each stage of the cycle and connect the part to its function? Rushing through the disassembly as a single activity loses the teaching value.

### 5. Diagnostic Thinking
**Developing well:** The student reads OBD-II codes and can articulate that the code points to a system and symptom, not a specific part. They can list multiple possible causes for a given code and propose a diagnostic sequence starting with the most likely and easiest to test.

**Developing slowly:** The student reads the code and immediately concludes "replace [part]" without considering alternative causes.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you presented the student with a code and walked through the full list of possible causes? The P0301 example in the student packet is a good teaching tool — a single code with five or more possible causes.
